Metadata.Coram: Chao Hick Tin JA; Woo Bih Li J; Yong Pung How CJ

Party_Details.Name: Andrea Heidi de Cruz

Facts.Text: Andrea de Cruz was a 27-year-old artiste with MediaCorp Studios when she suffered liver failure.
Around December 2001, during filming of the Chinese drama series "No Problem", Andrea came to know Rayson Tan and his wife Chen Liping and spent substantial time with them during filming.

From about December 2001, Andrea viewed TV Media Pte Ltd television commercials for the slimming product "Slim 10" that featured Chen Liping, including "before" and "after" images showing weight loss.

The Slim 10 television commercials Andrea saw were infomercials that stated that Chen Liping had tried the pills, described the pills as "100% herbal", and displayed TV Media Pte Ltd's name while emphasising that TV Media was the distributor of Slim 10.
Andrea observed life-sized cardboard cutouts and large posters of Chen Liping advertising Slim 10 at TV Media's retail outlet in Beach Road, Singapore.
Andrea continued to see Slim 10 advertisements on various television channels in Singapore up to late March 2002, before she travelled to Bangkok.
Andrea was aware that TV Media Pte Ltd was a large company whose products her family had previously purchased and whose advertisements she had seen in the United States.
Based on TV Media's size and reputation, Andrea formed the view that TV Media would have carried out necessary checks to ensure Slim 10 was safe before endorsing and marketing it extensively.
Andrea considered the reputation, reliability and credibility of health supplements important to her purchasing decisions and preferred products from entities known in Singapore.
In December 2001, Andrea told Rayson that she had seen Slim 10 advertisements and commented on Chen Liping's weight loss.
In response, Rayson told Andrea that the Slim 10 pills were effective and suggested that she take Slim 10 if she was concerned about her weight.

On 2002-01-11, Rayson handed Andrea two sets of Slim 10 pills packaged in clear unmarked test tubes and offered them at a price of $130 per bottle of 120 capsules, below the stated retail price of $149.90 per bottle.

Andrea purchased two bottles' worth of Slim 10 capsules from Rayson on 2002-01-11 and received the capsules in two clear, unmarked test tubes containing white or off-white capsules resembling those shown in TV Media's advertisements.

At the time of the first sale, Rayson informed Andrea that possible side effects of the pills included insomnia, chills and pain in her calves, and told her that apart from these, the pills were safe and all natural.
Around late January or early February 2002, after filming for "No Problem" was nearing completion, Andrea began consuming the first batch of Slim 10 capsules.

Andrea initially followed a regimen of four Slim 10 capsules three times a day, and after experiencing side effects such as insomnia, chills, calf pain and palpitations, she reduced her intake to four capsules in the morning, four at midday and two at night.
On 2002-02-15, Andrea sent a text message to Rayson stating that she had run out of "skinny pills".
On 2002-02-18, Andrea provided Rayson with a cheque for $260 corresponding to the cost of two further sets of Slim 10 pills.

On or about 2002-02-18, Rayson handed Andrea a second batch of approximately 240 Slim 10 capsules packed in unmarked aluminium foil packets and explained that he had requested this packaging because he kept the pills in his car and was concerned about heat.
Andrea began consuming the second batch of Slim 10 capsules in early to mid-March 2002 after finishing the first batch.
Around 2002-03-21, Andrea sent Rayson a text message asking him to help her obtain more Slim 10 pills as she was going on a holiday to Bangkok and wanted pills for maintenance.
On 2002-03-29, Andrea flew from Singapore to Bangkok for a holiday and during this trip she finished consuming the second batch of Slim 10 capsules.
On 2002-04-11, after returning from Bangkok, Andrea sent another text message to Rayson asking him to order more Slim 10 pills.
On 2002-04-14, Andrea's sister observed that Andrea's eyes appeared very yellow and jaundiced.

On 2002-04-15, Andrea consulted a doctor who informed her that her liver was inflamed and her eyes were very jaundiced, and she then went to Changi General Hospital where her liver inflammation was confirmed and she was hospitalised.
In the second week of her hospitalisation in April 2002, Andrea underwent a liver biopsy at Mount Alvernia Hospital.
On 2002-05-03, Dr Dede Selamat Sutedja from the National University Hospital liver team visited Andrea and prescribed medication, which she vomited.
On 2002-05-04, Andrea was transferred from Mount Alvernia Hospital to National University Hospital.

On 2002-05-05, Andrea developed encephalopathic symptoms, including inability to recognise people and incoherent muttering, and she was moved to the intensive care unit for immediate liver dialysis, after which her lucidity temporarily improved.

By early May 2002, Andrea's fiancé had been identified as the only suitable available liver donor, and the Ministry of Health granted special permission for an unrelated living donor liver transplant to proceed.
On 2002-05-07, Andrea was transferred to Gleneagles Hospital because the National University Hospital liver team had not previously performed a living donor liver transplant.
On 2002-05-07, Dr Tan Kai Chah at Gleneagles Hospital performed a living donor liver transplant operation on Andrea.
For approximately two and a half years before April 2002, Andrea had been consuming the health supplements Spirulina and Pycnogenol without reported medical problems.
On 2002-04-02, the Health Sciences Authority convened an urgent meeting with Health Biz Pte Ltd in response to reports from people who had consumed Slim 10 and were experiencing medical problems.

On 2002-04-03, Semon Liu and Health Biz Vice President Peter Boo sent a letter to the Health Sciences Authority appealing against a recall of Slim 10, stating that a recall would have devastating consequences for Health Biz.
On 2002-04-15, the Health Sciences Authority directed Health Biz Pte Ltd to suspend all sales of Slim 10 immediately and to withdraw the product from the Singapore market by 2002-04-29.
By mid-2002, laboratory tests on Slim 10 samples identified undeclared substances including nicotinamide, fenfluramine, thyroid gland extract and N-nitrosofenfluramine.
In June 2001, Health Biz Pte Ltd submitted an initial sample of Slim 10 to the Health Sciences Authority for product listing approval, accompanied by test reports from Setsco Services Pte Ltd.
On 2001-06-27, the Health Sciences Authority granted Health Biz Pte Ltd a licence for Slim 10 following its application and submission of test reports and a sample.

In July 2001, the Chinese manufacturer launched a new version of Slim 10 named "yue zhi tang jian fei jiao nang" and informed Health Biz that it was an improved version with the same ingredients and quantities as the original product.

In August 2001, the Health Sciences Authority queried Health Biz about differences in colour and smell between the new Slim 10 capsules, which contained brown coarse fragrant powder, and the original Slim 10 capsules, which contained smooth pale yellow powder.
Following the Health Sciences Authority's query in August 2001, Health Biz informed the Authority that future Slim 10 capsules would contain only the yellow powder.

On 2001-11-01, Health Biz sent a written undertaking to the Health Sciences Authority stating that it would ensure Slim 10 capsules did not contain poisons or synthetic substances and that it would submit test results for toxic heavy metals and microbial contamination within two months of importing each consignment.

On 2001-11-02, the Health Sciences Authority granted product listing approval for Slim 10 to Health Biz Pte Ltd, relying in part on Health Biz's written undertaking regarding testing for poisons and synthetic substances.
A distributorship agreement between Health Biz Pte Ltd and TV Media Pte Ltd for Slim 10 was originally dated 2001-03-01 and was signed by both parties around July 2001.
In mid-November 2001, TV Media proposed that the effective date of its distributorship agreement with Health Biz for Slim 10 be changed to commence on 2001-12-01, after product listing approval.

Before and after 2001-12-01, Semon arranged for Slim 10 capsules to be supplied to Rayson for distribution to MediaCorp artistes as part of product promotion and testing, with TV Media's knowledge and approval.

TV Media personnel, including Wilfred Wong, consumed Slim 10 capsules that arrived in various forms of unmarked packaging and different capsule colours, relying on the fact that the pills came directly from Semon Liu.
Health Biz imported Slim 10 capsules from a Chinese manufacturer in consignments that were not consistently batched or uniformly packaged, resulting in a variety of unmarked containers.

Health Biz provided surplus Slim 10 capsules from each imported batch to Rayson for distribution to his friends, relatives and colleagues, while round-number quantities were sent to DAC Pharmalab for repackaging into marked clear test tubes for TV Media's retail sales.
After product listing approval, Health Biz did not conduct poison and synthetic substance tests on subsequent consignments of Slim 10 before supplying them to TV Media and Rayson.

TV Media accepted consignments of Slim 10 from Health Biz for distribution and sale without independently verifying that Health Biz had conducted the testing it had undertaken to perform for each consignment.

In July and August 2002, Australian Federal Police Scientific Officer Keith Norman analysed three samples of Slim 10 and found that all contained brown powder, indicating that capsules with brown contents had been distributed despite Health Biz's earlier assurance to the Health Sciences Authority that only yellow-powder capsules would be supplied.

TV Media did not consult expert herbalists or legal experts about Slim 10 before distributing it and instead relied on internal internet research by staff member Wilfred Wong, who was not a herbal expert.

TV Media relied on representations from Health Biz Vice President Peter Boo that Health Biz already held a wholesale dealer's licence and that TV Media did not need to obtain its own wholesale dealer's licence for Slim 10 distribution.

Internal emails within TV Media prior to the dispute recorded discussions among staff expressing concerns about the safety of Slim 10 and about whether Semon was taking adequate steps to ensure product safety.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether, based on pre-dispute conduct and communications, Andrea's consumption of Slim 10 capsules distributed and promoted in Singapore was associated with subsequent severe liver injury and transplant, and whether the distributor and the importer-director were responsible sources of that risk exposure.
Party_Details.Rule: In negligence claims involving consumer products, liability assessment typically considers whether a product distributor or importer took reasonable steps to check the safety of products it marketed, whether its promotional statements about safety and composition were consistent with available information and testing, whether consumers foreseeably relied on those statements and the distributor's reputation when deciding to consume the product, and whether the product's characteristics and the timing and nature of the consumer's injury are consistent with the product being a factual cause of the injury.
Party_Details.Application: Andrea was a MediaCorp artiste who, from December 2001, was repeatedly exposed to Slim 10 infomercials and in-store advertising that prominently featured her colleague's wife, described the pills as "100% herbal", and identified TV Media as distributor. She knew TV Media as a large, established company and formed expectations that it would have checked product safety. Health Biz, controlled in practice by Semon Liu, imported Slim 10 from a Chinese manufacturer in loosely managed consignments, used DAC Pharmalab to repackage round-number quantities into marked test tubes for TV Media, and channelled surplus unmarked capsules to Rayson for distribution. TV Media staff, including Wilfred Wong, personally consumed Slim 10 from various unmarked packages, relying on Semon as the source, and TV Media knew that MediaCorp artistes were being supplied with unmarked samples. Health Biz gave the Health Sciences Authority an undertaking on 2001-11-01 to test each consignment for poisons and synthetic substances and to ensure only yellow-powder capsules were supplied, and product listing approval followed on 2001-11-02. Despite this, subsequent consignments were not batch-tested for poisons or synthetic adulterants before being supplied to TV Media and Rayson, and later forensic analysis in 2002 showed that distributed Slim 10 capsules contained brown powder and undeclared substances including nicotinamide, fenfluramine, thyroid gland extract and N-nitrosofenfluramine. TV Media did not independently verify Health Biz's compliance with its testing undertakings, did not obtain its own wholesale dealer's licence, and did not seek specialist toxicological or herbal advice, yet broadcast advertisements asserting that Slim 10 was "100% herbal" and safe. Andrea, who placed weight on the reputation of health products, bought Slim 10 from Rayson at a discount, in unmarked containers that visually resembled the advertised product, after Rayson described limited side effects and stated that the pills were natural and safe. She began consuming Slim 10 in late January or early February 2002, adjusted dosage in response to side effects such as insomnia, chills, calf pain and palpitations, obtained a second batch on 2002-02-18, and finished the second batch by the end of March 2002, including during a trip to Bangkok starting 2002-03-29. On 2002-04-14 her sister noticed jaundice, and on 2002-04-15 Andrea was found to have an inflamed liver and was hospitalised. Within weeks she developed encephalopathy, required intensive care and liver dialysis, and underwent a living donor liver transplant on 2002-05-07. She had been taking Spirulina and Pycnogenol for about two and a half years without prior liver issues. The temporal sequence between the start and cessation of Slim 10 ingestion and the onset of acute liver failure, the presence of undeclared pharmacologically active and nitrosamine compounds in Slim 10, the absence of comparable prior issues with her other supplements, and the pattern of Health Biz's and TV Media's handling, testing and promotion of Slim 10 together form the factual basis on which the court later evaluated duty, , causation and personal responsibility of the corporate actors.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The Court of Appeal affirmed the trial judge's findings that Andrea had in fact consumed Slim 10 capsules originating from Health Biz and distributed under TV Media's promotional umbrella, notwithstanding unmarked packaging and variant formulations. The court upheld the trial judge's acceptance of the late January/early February to late March/early April 2002 consumption window, relying heavily on Rayson Tan's evidence, contemporaneous text messages, and the 18 February 2002 cheque, and rejected earlier date ranges given in hospital histories as less reliable. Applying the Caparo framework, the court held that TV Media owed Andrea a duty of care as exclusive distributor and advertiser: damage to consumers from ingesting inadequately checked slimming pills was foreseeable; there was sufficient proximity because Andrea relied on TV Media's corporate reputation and advertising rather than on Health Biz, the Chinese manufacturer or Rayson; and it was fair, just and reasonable to impose a duty on a distributor that actively endorsed and promoted the product. The court distinguished economic loss cases like Hedley Byrne and emphasised that Donoghue v Stevenson and Watson v Buckley extend duty to distributors for physical injury from defective products. On breach, the court drew strong parallels with Watson v Buckley, finding that TV Media had been negligent in placing 'blind faith' in Semon and Health Biz. TV Media knew Semon had no pharmaceutical experience, did not investigate the Chinese manufacturer, did not insist on or review batch test reports, did not seek expert toxicological advice, and relied on HSA approval and internal internet research while advertising Slim 10 as '100% herbal' and 'safe'. The court rejected TV Media's attempt to shift responsibility to HSA, noting that HSA's role was to ensure minimum testing and that statutory undertakings and regulations placed ultimate responsibility for safety on importers and distributors. HSA's failure to detect fenfluramine did not absolve TV Media, and any potential HSA liability was academic as HSA was not a party. Internal TV Media emails expressing concern about Slim 10's safety and potential 'fallout' reinforced the conclusion that TV Media acted with disregard for consumer safety. On causation, the court rejected arguments of novus actus interveniens and contributory negligence based on Andrea's failure to stop taking Slim 10 when she experienced side effects, holding that her omission was part of the causal sequence rather than an independent cause, and noting that contributory negligence had not been pleaded below. The court carefully evaluated conflicting expert evidence from Drs Sutedja and Tan (for Andrea) and Drs Joyce and Wendon (for TV Media). It accepted that Andrea suffered fulminant liver failure and that the temporal relationship between Slim 10 ingestion and liver injury fell within the 5–90 day window described by Zimmerman for drug-induced hepatotoxicity, and within the 15–35 day post-cessation window consistent with drug causation. The court considered a 'worst-case' timing scenario and found it still compatible with Slim 10 as a cause. It discounted Spirulina as a likely cause given Andrea's long uneventful use and the paucity of evidence of Spirulina hepatotoxicity, and noted that N-nitrosofenfluramine is a nitrosamine, a class generally regarded as hepatotoxic, with Japanese animal studies indicating liver damage. The court accepted circumstantial causation reasoning akin to Grant v Australian Knitting Mills, concluding that the totality of evidence, including another local fatal Slim 10 case, pointed strongly to Slim 10 as the highly probable cause of Andrea's massive hepatocellular necrosis, with other explanations being highly improbable. It treated the Maria and Victorino and Naranjo clinical scales as crude guides of limited applicability to a new drug and held that they did not displace the overall clinical and temporal picture. On Semon Liu's personal liability, the court reiterated the Salomon principle and the general rule that directors are not automatically liable for company torts, but applied the exception for directors who 'authorise, direct or procure' torts, as in Performing Right Society and subsequent authorities. It rejected Semon's argument that the phrase only covers single positive acts and not negligent omissions, citing Bankes LJ's statement that authorisation can be inferred from omissions and Denning LJ's criticism of the commission/omission distinction. The court held that Andrea's pleading that Semon authorised, directed and/or procured Health Biz's negligence was effectively a plea to pierce the corporate veil. Factually, the court found that Semon was Health Biz's founder, president, 99% shareholder and controlling mind; that Peter Boo, despite his title, functioned as an employee and assistant; and that Semon personally handled all significant dealings with the Chinese manufacturer and HSA, including decisions about adopting the new formulation, responding to HSA's concerns about capsule colour and texture, and appealing against the recall. Only Semon had contact with the manufacturer and could have ensured adherence to yellow-powder-only supply and proper batching. The court accepted the trial judge's characterisation that Semon's involvement in Health Biz's negligence was 'total' and that nothing of importance occurred without his knowledge or direction. It held that Semon had in fact directed the negligent failure to implement proper batching and testing and to seek professional interpretation of test reports, and that had proper testing and interpretation occurred, the presence of banned substances such as fenfluramine would have been detected, Slim 10 would not have been marketed, and Andrea would not have consumed it. The court therefore found a sufficient causal link between Semon's conduct and Andrea's injury. On veil piercing, the court weighed the policy tension between limited liability and preventing directors from using the corporate form to shield personal wrongdoing. Applying Gabriel Peter, it focused on Semon's level of involvement and concluded that he was effectively Health Biz's alter ego. In the 'very exceptional' circumstances of this case, it held that Health Biz's corporate veil should be lifted and Semon held personally liable as a joint tortfeasor for authorising, directing and/or procuring Health Biz's negligent acts. Regarding apportionment, the court applied Dingle v Associated Newspapers and Chuang Uming, holding that where multiple tortfeasors cause indivisible injury, each proximate cause is liable for the whole damage as between plaintiff and defendant, with contribution issues to be resolved inter se. It analogised to Wong Jin Fah, where overlapping omissions by multiple defendants led to joint and several liability. TV Media's attempt to characterise Semon's conduct as more blameworthy and its own role as merely ancillary advertising was rejected; TV Media's omissions in checking safety and its active promotion were proximate causes of the same indivisible liver injury. The court declined to interfere with the trial judge's decision to impose joint and several liability, noting that appellate interference with apportionment is reserved for very exceptional cases. On quantum, the court reviewed the trial judge's award of $250,000 for pain, suffering and loss of amenities and a multiplier of 34 for future medical expenses. It reiterated that damages in personal injury cases are compensatory, not punitive, and that an appellate court will only adjust an award if the judge applied a wrong principle or the amount is manifestly excessive or inadequate. The court accepted that the judge properly considered both Andrea's past suffering—36 days of hospitalisation, numerous invasive procedures, encephalopathy, and a major transplant—and her lifelong consequences, including permanent immunosuppression, increased risks of renal failure and skin cancer, restrictions on physical activity and diet, reproductive risks, frequent hospital follow-up, and uninsurability. It rejected comparisons to lower awards in other injury categories as inapt given the absence of local precedents for liver transplant cases and the severity and uniqueness of Andrea's condition. The court therefore upheld the general damages and the multiplier, and dismissed the appeals of TV Media and Semon on both liability and quantum, confirming that TV Media and Semon (via Health Biz) were liable in negligence for Andrea's liver failure and its consequences.

Metadata.Coram: Chao Hick Tin JA; Woo Bih Li J; Yong Pung How CJ

Party_Details.Name: TV Media Pte Ltd
Party_Details.Law_Firm: B Rao and K S Rajah

Facts.Text: TV Media Pte Ltd is a Singapore company engaged in the distribution, advertising and retail of consumer products, including the slimming product Slim 10.

A distributorship agreement between Health Biz Pte Ltd and TV Media Pte Ltd for Slim 10 was originally dated 2001-03-01 and was signed by both parties around July 2001, appointing TV Media as the sole distributor of Slim 10 in Singapore.
From mid-2001 onwards, TV Media prepared to market Slim 10 in Singapore based on information and samples provided by Health Biz Pte Ltd and its director, Semon Liu.
TV Media relied on representations from Health Biz that Slim 10 had obtained the necessary regulatory approvals and that each batch would be tested for safety.

TV Media did not conduct independent background checks on the Chinese manufacturer of Slim 10 beyond information provided by Health Biz and did not engage external pharmaceutical or toxicology experts before commencing distribution.

TV Media did not obtain its own wholesale dealer's licence for Slim 10 distribution and instead relied on Health Biz Vice President Peter Boo's statement that Health Biz's existing wholesale dealer's licence was sufficient.
TV Media accepted consignments of Slim 10 capsules from Health Biz for wholesale distribution and retail sale without requiring batch-specific test reports for poisons or synthetic adulterants.

TV Media did not implement an internal procedure to verify that each consignment of Slim 10 supplied by Health Biz complied with Health Biz's undertaking to the Health Sciences Authority regarding testing for poisons and synthetic substances.

TV Media developed and broadcast Slim 10 television infomercials featuring MediaCorp artiste Chen Liping, showing before-and-after weight loss images and describing Slim 10 as "100% herbal" and safe for consumption, after obtaining Health Sciences Authority approval for the advertisement wording.
TV Media's Slim 10 advertisements prominently displayed TV Media's name and identified TV Media as the distributor of Slim 10 in Singapore.
TV Media installed life-sized cardboard cutouts and large posters of Chen Liping promoting Slim 10 at its retail outlet in Beach Road, Singapore.
TV Media allowed Slim 10 to be sold at its Beach Road retail outlet in marked clear test tubes repackaged by DAC Pharmalab from bulk capsules supplied by Health Biz.
TV Media was aware that Health Biz was providing Slim 10 capsules in unmarked packaging to MediaCorp artistes, including through Rayson Tan, for product testing and promotion.

TV Media staff member Wilfred Wong and another staff member personally consumed Slim 10 capsules that arrived in various unmarked packages and different capsule colours, relying on the fact that the capsules came directly from Semon Liu.

After Health Biz obtained product listing approval for Slim 10 on 2001-11-02, TV Media continued to distribute Slim 10 without requesting evidence that Health Biz was conducting batch tests for poisons and synthetic substances as undertaken to the Health Sciences Authority.

In mid-November 2001, TV Media proposed that the effective date of its distributorship agreement with Health Biz be changed to 2001-12-01, after product listing approval, while Slim 10 distribution to MediaCorp artistes through Rayson had already commenced.

TV Media relied on internal internet research by Wilfred Wong regarding Slim 10 and its ingredients, despite his lack of specialist herbal or toxicological expertise, instead of commissioning independent scientific evaluation.

Internal emails within TV Media before April 2002 recorded staff expressing concerns about the safety of Slim 10 and about whether Semon Liu and Health Biz were taking adequate steps to ensure product safety, as well as concerns about potential reputational fallout for TV Media.

Following the Health Sciences Authority's 2002-04-15 directive to suspend Slim 10 sales and withdraw the product by 2002-04-29, TV Media ceased Slim 10 distribution in compliance with the regulatory order.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether TV Media Pte Ltd, as exclusive distributor and advertiser of Slim 10 in Singapore, failed to exercise reasonable care in checking the safety and composition of Slim 10 before promoting it as "100% herbal" and safe, and whether its promotional activities and distribution were a factual cause of Andrea's consumption of Slim 10 and subsequent liver injury.
Party_Details.Rule: A product distributor that markets a consumable product to the public may be take reasonable steps to verify the product's safety and composition, to deal with reputable suppliers, and to ensure that its advertising claims about safety and ingredients are supported by appropriate testing and regulatory compliance. Where consumers foreseeably rely on the distributor's reputation and representations, and the product causes physical injury, the distributor can be held in negligence if its conduct falls below the standard of a reasonable distributor and its acts or omissions are a proximate cause of the injury.
Party_Details.Application: TV Media entered into a distributorship arrangement with Health Biz to act as the sole distributor of Slim 10 in Singapore and began planning marketing activities based on information from Health Biz and its director, Semon Liu. TV Media did not independently investigate the Chinese manufacturer or Health Biz's quality control systems, despite knowing that Semon had no prior experience in importing Chinese proprietary medicines. It relied on Health Biz's regulatory approvals and undertakings to the Health Sciences Authority, and on internal internet research by a non-expert employee, instead of commissioning independent toxicological or herbal assessments. TV Media did not obtain its own wholesale dealer's licence and accepted consignments of Slim 10 without requiring batch-specific test reports for poisons or synthetic adulterants, even after Health Biz had formally undertaken to test each consignment and to ensure only yellow-powder capsules were supplied. TV Media knew that Slim 10 capsules were being supplied in various unmarked packages to MediaCorp artistes, including through Rayson Tan, and that its own staff were consuming capsules from unmarked packaging based solely on their origin from Semon. Despite this, TV Media created and broadcast infomercials and in-store advertising that described Slim 10 as "100% herbal" and safe for consumption, prominently associating TV Media's name and reputation with the product. Andrea, a MediaCorp artiste, saw these advertisements repeatedly, observed Slim 10 promotional materials at TV Media's Beach Road outlet, and knew TV Media as a large, established company whose products her family had previously purchased. She formed expectations that TV Media would have ensured Slim 10's safety before endorsing it and decided to obtain Slim 10 through her colleague Rayson, who was closely linked to the advertising campaign via his wife, the featured endorser. The capsules Andrea received from Rayson visually resembled those in TV Media's advertisements, and she relied on the combination of TV Media's corporate backing and Rayson’s assurances when deciding to consume them. Later forensic testing showed that Slim 10 capsules distributed in Singapore contained undeclared pharmacologically active and nitrosamine compounds, and Andrea developed acute liver failure within a timeframe consistent with drug-induced hepatotoxicity after consuming two batches of Slim 10. TV Media's failure to verify Health Biz's testing practices, its acceptance of unverified consignments, and its strong promotional claims about safety and herbal composition formed part of the causal chain leading to Andrea's decision to consume Slim 10 and her subsequent injury, as later recognised by the court in finding that TV Media owed and breached a duty of care and that its caused Andrea's liver failure.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The Court of Appeal upheld the trial judge's conclusion that TV Media owed Andrea a duty of care, breached that duty, and was a proximate cause of her liver failure. Applying Caparo, the court found that harm to consumers from inadequately checked slimming pills was clearly foreseeable, that there was sufficient proximity because Andrea relied on TV Media's advertising and corporate reputation rather than on Health Biz or the Chinese manufacturer, and that it was fair, just and reasonable to impose a duty on a distributor that actively endorsed and promoted the product. The court rejected TV Media's argument that Andrea fell into an 'unknown and unlimited' class of persons akin to economic loss cases, distinguishing Hedley Byrne and emphasising that this was a physical injury case governed by principles from Donoghue v Stevenson and Watson v Buckley, which extend duty to distributors. On breach, the court held that TV Media's conduct fell below the standard of a reasonable distributor. It noted that TV Media knew Semon had no pharmaceutical experience, did not investigate the Chinese manufacturer, did not insist on or review batch test reports, did not seek expert advice on the Setsco reports or on Slim 10's composition, and relied instead on HSA's limited approval and internal internet research. TV Media accepted consignments without verifying compliance with Health Biz's undertaking to test each consignment and to supply only yellow-powder capsules, and it knew that Slim 10 was being distributed in various unmarked packages to MediaCorp artistes. Despite this, TV Media advertised Slim 10 as '100% herbal' and 'safe for consumption', closely associating its name with the product. The court rejected TV Media's attempt to shift responsibility to HSA, accepting evidence from HSA's Dr Bloodworth that HSA's role was to ensure minimum testing and that importers remained responsible for product safety. It held that statutory requirements for importers to submit declarations and test results, and Health Biz's written undertaking, showed that HSA approval did not guarantee safety and did not relieve distributors of their own obligations. Internal TV Media emails expressing doubts about Slim 10's safety and concern about reputational 'fallout' were treated as strong evidence that TV Media suspected safety issues yet continued to rely on Semon without instituting adequate checks. On causation, the court accepted the trial judge's reasoning that if TV Media had insisted on seeing batch test reports and obtaining professional interpretation, it would not have made unqualified safety claims, and Andrea would not have been induced to take Slim 10. The court rejected TV Media's novus actus and contributory negligence arguments based on Andrea's failure to stop taking the pills when she experienced side effects, holding that her omission was not a wholly independent cause and that contributory negligence had not been pleaded. On medical causation, the court endorsed the trial judge's reliance on Zimmerman’s temporal criteria, the timing of Andrea's consumption and liver failure, the presence of undeclared compounds including N-nitrosofenfluramine, and the exclusion of other plausible causes such as Spirulina and viral hepatitis. It accepted that circumstantial evidence, as in Grant v Australian Knitting Mills, could establish causation on a balance of probabilities and concluded that Slim 10 was the highly probable cause of Andrea's liver failure. The court dismissed TV Media's arguments based on clinical scales (Naranjo, Maria and Victorino), treating them as crude guides of limited value for a new drug, and found that they did not outweigh the overall clinical and temporal picture. On apportionment, the court applied Dingle and Chuang Uming, holding that TV Media was a proximate cause of an indivisible injury and therefore jointly and severally liable for the whole damage as between Andrea and each tortfeasor. It analogised to Wong Jin Fah, where overlapping omissions by multiple defendants led to joint and several liability, and rejected TV Media's contention that Semon's conduct was so much more blameworthy as to warrant a different apportionment. The court also rejected TV Media's attempt to characterise Andrea's continued consumption as a more proximate cause. On quantum, the court declined to interfere with the trial judge's award of $250,000 for pain and suffering and loss of amenities and a multiplier of 34 for future medical expenses, finding no error of principle and no manifest excess. It noted the absence of local comparators for liver transplant cases and accepted the trial judge's assessment of Andrea's extensive past suffering and lifelong consequences. Accordingly, TV Media's appeal on both liability and quantum was dismissed.

Metadata.Coram: Chao Hick Tin JA; Woo Bih Li J; Yong Pung How CJ

Party_Details.Name: Semon Liu

Facts.Text: Semon Liu was a director and principal shareholder of Health Biz Pte Ltd, a Singapore company that imported and sold the slimming product Slim 10.
From its inception, Health Biz Pte Ltd had Semon Liu as its founder, director and president, and he held approximately 99% of the company's shares.
Before employing Vice President Peter Boo, Semon decided that Health Biz would import Slim 10 from a Chinese manufacturer and entered into arrangements with that manufacturer.
Semon personally negotiated with the Chinese manufacturer of Slim 10, including agreeing on terms of supply and making payments in cash during trips to China.
In July 2001, Semon attended the Chinese manufacturer's launch of a new version of Slim 10 named "yue zhi tang jian fei jiao nang" and decided that Health Biz would import this new version.

Semon arranged for samples of the original Slim 10 formulation to be sent to Setsco Services Pte Ltd for laboratory testing as part of Health Biz's application to the Health Sciences Authority for import and wholesale dealer licences.
Health Biz, under Semon's direction, submitted Setsco test reports and a sample of Slim 10 to the Health Sciences Authority in June 2001 as part of its application for product listing approval.

After the Health Sciences Authority queried Health Biz in August 2001 about differences in colour and smell between the new and original Slim 10 capsules, Semon travelled to China to obtain a written explanation from the manufacturer regarding the disparity.

Following the Health Sciences Authority's August 2001 query, Health Biz informed the Authority that future Slim 10 capsules would contain only the yellow powder, a representation made while Semon was the only person dealing directly with the manufacturer.

On 2001-11-01, Health Biz sent a letter of undertaking to the Health Sciences Authority stating that it would ensure Slim 10 capsules did not contain poisons or synthetic substances and that it would submit test results for toxic heavy metals and microbial contamination within two months of importing each consignment.

After product listing approval on 2001-11-02, Health Biz, under Semon's control, did not implement a system to batch and test each consignment of Slim 10 for poisons and synthetic adulterants before distribution.

Health Biz imported Slim 10 capsules from the Chinese manufacturer in consignments that were not consistently batched or uniformly packaged, resulting in a variety of unmarked containers being received in Singapore.

Health Biz sent round-number quantities of Slim 10 capsules from each consignment to DAC Pharmalab for repackaging into marked clear test tubes for TV Media's retail sales and provided surplus unmarked capsules to Rayson Tan for distribution to his friends, relatives and colleagues.

Semon approached MediaCorp artistes Rayson Tan and Chen Liping to promote Slim 10, presenting his personal portfolio and curriculum vitae and describing his personal investment in the Chinese manufacturer.

Semon represented to TV Media personnel, including Wilfred Wong, that he had invested his personal savings into the Chinese manufacturer of Slim 10 and had close relationships with the factory owner and his son.
Semon authorised the supply of Slim 10 capsules to Rayson Tan for distribution among MediaCorp artistes as part of product promotion and testing, with TV Media's knowledge.
On 2002-04-02, Semon attended or was represented at an urgent meeting with the Health Sciences Authority convened because consumers who had taken Slim 10 were reporting medical problems.

On 2002-04-03, Semon and Health Biz Vice President Peter Boo sent a letter to the Health Sciences Authority appealing against the recall of Slim 10, stating that a recall would have devastating consequences for Health Biz.

On the night of 2002-04-03, Semon reviewed and edited multiple drafts of the appeal letter to the Health Sciences Authority prepared by Peter Boo and Wilfred Wong before approving the final version for submission.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether, in his capacity as controlling director and principal shareholder of Health Biz Pte Ltd, Semon Liu personally authorised, directed or procured Health Biz's negligent course of conduct in importing, batching, testing and supplying Slim 10, such that the corporate veil should be lifted and he should be held personally as a joint tortfeasor for Andrea's injury.
Party_Details.Rule: Company law generally treats a company as a separate legal person from its directors and shareholders, so that proof of a company's tort does not automatically establish personal liability of its directors. However, a director may be personally as a joint tortfeasor where he personally participates in, authorises, directs or procures the commission of the tort, including through a sustained course of conduct or omissions. In deciding whether to disregard the corporate veil and fix a director with personal liability, courts consider the factual extent of the director's control and involvement in the company's acts and whether, in the circumstances, it is appropriate to treat the director as having effectively committed the tort alongside the company.
Party_Details.Application: Health Biz Pte Ltd, the importer and seller of Slim 10, was effectively controlled by Semon Liu, who founded the company, served as its president and director, and held about 99% of its shares. He decided that Health Biz would import Slim 10, negotiated directly with the Chinese manufacturer, attended the launch of the new formulation, and personally travelled to China to pay for consignments in cash and to obtain explanations when the Health Sciences Authority queried differences in capsule colour and texture. Health Biz's written undertaking to the Health Sciences Authority on 2001-11-01, promising to ensure that Slim 10 capsules were free of poisons and synthetic substances and to submit test results for each consignment, was given while Semon was the only person dealing with the manufacturer and overseeing imports. After product listing approval, Health Biz imported Slim 10 in a haphazard manner, without a clear batching system, and did not implement the promised regime of testing each consignment for poisons and synthetic adulterants before supplying capsules to TV Media and Rayson. Round-number quantities were sent to DAC Pharmalab for repackaging into marked test tubes, while surplus unmarked capsules were given to Rayson for distribution to his network, including MediaCorp artistes. Semon personally promoted Slim 10 to Rayson and Chen Liping, emphasising his personal investment in the manufacturer and his close relationship with the factory owners, and authorised the supply of capsules to Rayson for promotional distribution. Within Health Biz, Vice President Peter Boo handled administrative tasks and liaised with the Health Sciences Authority, but evidence showed that Peter reported to Semon and that Semon made key decisions, such as instructing Peter to draft and submit the 2002-04-03 appeal letter against the Slim 10 recall and personally vetting and editing multiple drafts before approval. No evidence indicated that any other Health Biz director or shareholder directed the importation, batching or testing practices. Later forensic analysis revealed that Slim 10 capsules distributed in Singapore contained brown powder and undeclared substances, including fenfluramine and N-nitrosofenfluramine, contrary to Health Biz's assurance that only yellow-powder capsules would be supplied. The absence of a proper batching and testing system, and the failure to seek professional interpretation of test reports, were operational decisions within Health Biz that, in practice, depended on Semon's direction and oversight. These pre-dispute facts formed the basis for the court's later conclusion that Semon had personally authorised, directed and procured Health Biz's negligent handling of Slim 10 and that, given his total control and involvement, the corporate veil should be lifted to impose personal liability.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The Court of Appeal affirmed the trial judge's decision to hold Semon Liu personally liable as a joint tortfeasor for Health Biz's negligence. It began by restating the Salomon principle that a company is a separate legal entity and that directors are not automatically liable for company torts, citing Rainham Chemical Works and Gabriel Peter. It then applied the established exception that directors who order or procure tortious acts by the company may be liable as joint tortfeasors, as in Performing Right Society. The court rejected Semon's argument that Andrea's claim for having 'authorised, directed and/or procured' Health Biz's negligence was conceptually distinct from veil piercing; it held that such a claim effectively invites the court to look through the corporate veil to fix a director with personal liability. On the meaning of 'authorised, directed and/or procured', Semon contended that the phrase applied only to single, positive acts (such as copyright infringement) and not to omissions forming a negligent course of conduct. The court rejected this narrow interpretation, relying on Bankes LJ's statement in Performing Right Society that authorisation or permission can be inferred from acts falling short of direct positive acts, including indifference manifested by omissions. It also noted that negligence doctrine has long encompassed both acts and omissions, citing Blyth and Donoghue v Stevenson, and endorsed Denning LJ's view in Hawkins that the commission/omission distinction is often illusory. The court observed that Health Biz's alleged omissions (failure to test, failure to batch properly) could equally be characterised as positive acts of selling untested and improperly batched pills. It further pointed out that Andrea's pleading against Health Biz was framed in positive terms, alleging that Health Biz imported, distributed and sold pills containing poisonous or harmful substances. Turning to the facts, the court endorsed the trial judge's finding that Peter Boo, despite his title as Vice President and director, functioned essentially as an employee and assistant, while Semon was the 'one constant director and shareholder' and the controlling mind of Health Biz. It noted that Semon decided to import Slim 10, signed the contract with the Chinese manufacturer, and only thereafter brought Peter into the company as a personal assistant. Semon alone dealt with the manufacturer, travelled to China to pay for consignments, attended the launch of the new formulation, and obtained written explanations when the Health Sciences Authority queried differences in capsule colour and texture. When Health Biz assured the Authority that future capsules would contain only yellow powder, Semon was the only person in a position to ensure that the manufacturer complied. The court found that Semon could have instructed the manufacturer to implement a proper batching system and to adhere to the agreed formulation, but did not do so. The court also highlighted Semon's central role in regulatory interactions: Health Biz's letter of undertaking to the Health Sciences Authority, promising to test each consignment for poisons and synthetic substances and to submit test results, was given under his leadership; when the Authority decided to recall Slim 10, Semon decided to appeal and instructed Peter to draft the appeal letter, personally vetting and editing several drafts before approving the final version. Evidence from TV Media's Wilfred Wong confirmed that Semon had to approve the appeal letter before submission. The court concluded that Semon's involvement in Health Biz's negligent acts was 'not merely very great, but total', and that 'nothing was done without his knowledge'. On causation, Semon argued that even if he had authorised or directed Health Biz's negligence, those acts did not cause Andrea's injury because, he said, tests had been done and N-nitrosofenfluramine was unknown at the time. The court read the trial judge's comment that 'only [Semon] could have decided that a general screening for poisons was not necessary' in context, clarifying that the criticism was directed at the failure to test each consignment and to seek professional interpretation of test results, not at the absence of any testing. It accepted the trial judge's reasoning that, had proper batching and testing been implemented and had professional advice been sought on the test reports, the presence of banned substances such as fenfluramine would have been detected, Slim 10 would not have been marketed, and Andrea would not have consumed it. The fact that N-nitrosofenfluramine was not then recognised did not break the causal chain, because the detection of other prohibited substances would have led to withdrawal of the product. On veil piercing, the court considered policy concerns: while recognising the commercial importance of limited liability, it stressed that directors should not be able to use the corporate form to shield themselves from personal responsibility for torts they effectively commit. Citing Gabriel Peter, it held that whether a director is personally liable depends on the factual extent of his involvement and whether he is effectively the company's alter ego. In Semon's case, the court found that he was clearly the controlling mind and spirit of Health Biz, that he personally orchestrated the importation, batching and distribution of Slim 10, and that his involvement in the negligent course of conduct was exceptional in its completeness. It therefore held that the 'very exceptional circumstances' of the case justified lifting Health Biz's corporate veil and imposing personal liability on Semon for authorising, directing and procuring Health Biz's negligent acts. The court rejected Semon's policy argument that such a ruling would create 'open hunting season' on small companies, emphasising that its decision was grounded in the specific and unusual facts and did not establish a general rule that dominant directors are always personally liable. It also dismissed Semon's attempt to minimise his role as a 'miniscule part' of a complex causal chain, reiterating that his decisions on batching, testing and distribution were central to the events leading to Andrea's injury. Accordingly, the court dismissed Semon's appeal on liability, holding him jointly and severally liable with Health Biz and TV Media for Andrea's damages, and also rejected his challenges to the quantum of damages for the same reasons given in relation to TV Media.
